metadata.deck_subtitle: Essential Words for Your China Trip
metadata.description: Curated focus words from all chapters plus travel essentials — the words you need most for hiking, eating, shopping, and navigating in Shanghai, Chongqing, and Zhangjiajie

content.name: good
content.side_e: Nǐ hǎo! Jīntiān tiānqì hěn hǎo.
content.side_f: Hello! The weather today is great.
content.side_g: The most essential Chinese word. 你好 is 'hello.' 好的 (hǎo de) means 'okay/alright' — use it to confirm. 好吃 'delicious,' 好喝 'good to drink,' 好看 'good-looking.' Source: Chapter 1.

content.name: please
content.side_e: Qǐng jìn! Qǐng zuò!
content.side_f: Please come in! Please sit!
content.side_g: Use before any request to be polite. 请问 (qǐngwèn) means 'excuse me, may I ask...' — your go-to phrase for asking strangers for help. 请给我... means 'please give me...' Source: Chapter 1.

content.name: thank
content.side_e: Xièxie nǐ de bāngzhù!
content.side_f: Thank you for your help!
content.side_g: Say this constantly. 谢谢你 is slightly more emphatic than just 谢谢. The response is usually 不客气 (bú kèqi, 'you're welcome') or 不用谢 (búyòng xiè, 'no need to thank'). Source: Chapter 3.

content.name: i_me
content.side_e: Wǒ shì Měiguó rén. Wǒ lái lǚyóu de.
content.side_f: I'm American. I'm here for travel.
content.side_g: 我们 (wǒmen) means 'we/us.' 我的 (wǒ de) means 'my/mine.' 给我 (gěi wǒ) means 'give me.' Source: Chapter 1.

content.name: want_will
content.side_e: Wǒ yào yì wǎn suānlàtāng.
content.side_f: I want a bowl of hot and sour soup.
content.side_g: Essential for ordering food and expressing desires. 我要这个 (wǒ yào zhège) means 'I want this one' — great for pointing at menu items. 不要 (búyào) means 'don't want.' 要不要？means 'do you want (it)?' Source: Chapter 6.

content.name: not_no
content.side_e: Bù, wǒ bú yào. Bú shì zhège.
content.side_f: No, I don't want it. It's not this one.
content.side_g: The basic negation word. Changes to 2nd tone (bú) before 4th-tone words: 不是 (bú shì), 不要 (búyào). Use 没 (méi) instead for past tense or with 有. Source: Chapter 1.

content.name: this
content.side_e: Wǒ yào zhège. Zhège duōshao qián?
content.side_f: I want this one. How much is this?
content.side_g: Point and say 这个 (zhège, 'this one') — works everywhere. 这里/这儿 (zhèlǐ/zhèr) means 'here.' In spoken Chinese, 这个 is often pronounced 'zhèige.' Source: Chapter 2.

content.name: that
content.side_e: Nàge fànguǎnr hěn hǎo. Wǒmen qù nàr ba.
content.side_f: That restaurant is great. Let's go there.
content.side_g: 那个 (nàge/nèige, 'that one') for pointing at things farther away. 那里/那儿 (nàlǐ/nàr) means 'there.' Source: Chapter 2.

content.name: what
content.side_e: Zhè shì shénme? Nǐ yào chī shénme?
content.side_f: What is this? What do you want to eat?
content.side_g: Goes where the answer would be in the sentence — no need to rearrange word order like in English. 什么时候 (shénme shíhou) means 'when.' Source: Chapter 1.

content.name: how
content.side_e: Qù Zhāngjiājiè zěnme zǒu?
content.side_f: How do I get to Zhangjiajie?
content.side_g: Use to ask 'how' or express surprise at something. 怎么走 (zěnme zǒu) means 'how to get there' — essential for asking directions. 怎么样 (zěnmeyàng) means 'how about it?' or 'how is it?' Source: Chapter 7.

content.name: go
content.side_e: Wǒmen qù pá shān ba!
content.side_f: Let's go hiking!
content.side_g: One of the most used verbs. 去 + place: 去饭馆 (go to a restaurant). 去 + verb: 去看 (go see), 去买 (go buy). Add 吧 at the end for suggestions: 去吧 (let's go). Source: Chapter 4.

content.name: come
content.side_e: Wǒ cóng Měiguó lái de.
content.side_f: I came from America.
content.side_g: Opposite of 去. Also used as a casual way to order: 来一个这个 (lái yí ge zhège, 'I'll have one of this'). People will ask 你从哪里来？(Nǐ cóng nǎlǐ lái? 'Where are you from?'). Source: Chapter 5.

content.name: eat
content.side_e: Wǒmen qù chī huǒguō ba!
content.side_f: Let's go eat hot pot!
content.side_g: 吃饭 (chīfàn) means 'eat a meal.' 吃了吗？(Chī le ma?) is a casual greeting meaning 'have you eaten?' You can say 吃不了 (chī bu liǎo) meaning 'I can't eat (any more).' Source: Chapter 3.

content.name: at_located
content.side_e: Cèsuǒ zài nǎr? Wǒ zài zhèr.
content.side_f: Where is the restroom? I'm here.
content.side_g: Dual use as verb ('to be at') and preposition ('at/in'). ...在哪儿？(zài nǎr) is 'where is...?' — one of the most useful travel patterns. Source: Chapter 6.

content.name: how_many_much
content.side_e: Zhège duōshao qián?
content.side_f: How much is this?
content.side_g: 多少钱？(duōshao qián) is 'how much money?' — you'll use this in every shop and market. Unlike 几, 多少 is for any quantity and doesn't need a measure word. Source: Chapter 9.

content.name: money
content.side_e: Wǒ méi dài qián, kěyǐ shuā kǎ ma?
content.side_f: I didn't bring money, can I pay by card?
content.side_g: In China, most people pay with WeChat Pay or Alipay. 现金 (xiànjīn) means 'cash.' You might need to say 可以用现金吗？(kěyǐ yòng xiànjīn ma, 'can I use cash?'). Source: Chapter 9.

content.name: buy
content.side_e: Wǒ xiǎng mǎi piào.
content.side_f: I want to buy a ticket.
content.side_g: 买东西 (mǎi dōngxi) means 'buy things/go shopping.' 买票 (mǎi piào) means 'buy a ticket' — for subway, trains, scenic areas. Don't confuse with 卖 (mài, 'to sell'). Source: Chapter 9.

content.name: cheap
content.side_e: Kěyǐ piányi yìdiǎn ma?
content.side_f: Can it be a little cheaper?
content.side_g: Essential for market haggling. 便宜一点 (piányi yìdiǎn, 'a bit cheaper') is the classic bargaining phrase. 太贵了 (tài guì le, 'too expensive') pairs well with this. Source: Chapter 9.

content.name: subway
content.side_e: Zuò dìtiě qù, hěn fāngbiàn.
content.side_f: Take the subway there, it's very convenient.
content.side_g: Shanghai and Chongqing both have extensive subway systems. 地铁站 (dìtiě zhàn) means 'subway station.' Lines are numbered: 一号线 (yī hào xiàn, 'Line 1'). Source: Chapter 10.

content.name: taxi
content.side_e: Wǒmen dǎ chē qù fēijīchǎng ba.
content.side_f: Let's take a taxi to the airport.
content.side_g: 打车 (dǎ chē) is the colloquial way to say 'take a taxi.' In China, use DiDi (滴滴, Dīdī) app to hail rides — like Uber. 出租车 (chūzūchē) is the shorter form you'll hear more often. Source: Chapter 10.

content.name: walk
content.side_a: To walk; to go; to leave
content.side_e: Zǒu lù qù, dàgài shí fēnzhōng.
content.side_f: Walk there, about ten minutes.
content.side_g: 走路 (zǒu lù) means 'walk/on foot.' 怎么走？(Zěnme zǒu?) means 'how do I get there?' — essential for asking directions. 走吧 (zǒu ba) means 'let's go.' Source: Chapter 10.

content.name: airplane
content.side_e: Wǒ zuò fēijī cóng Shànghǎi dào Chóngqìng.
content.side_f: I'm flying from Shanghai to Chongqing.
content.side_g: 飞机场 (fēijīchǎng) means 'airport.' 坐飞机 (zuò fēijī) means 'take a plane.' You'll likely fly between Shanghai, Chongqing, and Zhangjiajie. Source: Chapter 10.

content.name: cell_phone
content.side_e: Wǒ yòng shǒujī kàn dìtú.
content.side_f: I use my phone to look at the map.
content.side_g: Essential device in China — mobile payment (手机支付, shǒujī zhīfù) is everywhere. 手机没电了 (shǒujī méi diàn le) means 'phone is dead' — important to avoid. Source: Chapter 10.

content.name: restaurant
content.side_e: Fùjìn yǒu shénme hǎo de fànguǎnr?
content.side_f: Are there any good restaurants nearby?
content.side_g: 饭馆 is a casual restaurant. 餐厅 (cāntīng) is more formal. You can ask locals 附近有什么好吃的？(Fùjìn yǒu shénme hǎochī de? 'What's good to eat nearby?'). Source: Chapter 12.

content.name: order_food
content.side_e: Wǒmen diǎn cài ba. Nǐ xiǎng chī shénme?
content.side_f: Let's order. What do you want to eat?
content.side_g: 点 means 'to select/point' and 菜 means 'dishes.' At a restaurant, the waiter may ask 你们点菜吗？(Nǐmen diǎn cài ma? 'Ready to order?'). Source: Chapter 12.

content.name: waiter
content.side_e: Fúwùyuán! Qǐng lái yí xià.
content.side_f: Waiter! Please come here a moment.
content.side_g: Call out 服务员！to get a server's attention — it's normal and not rude in China. Works for male and female staff. Source: Chapter 12.

content.name: hungry
content.side_e: Wǒ è le! Wǒmen qù chīfàn ba.
content.side_f: I'm hungry! Let's go eat.
content.side_g: 饿了 (è le) means 'became hungry.' 很饿 (hěn è) means 'very hungry.' 饿死了 (è sǐ le) is an exaggeration meaning 'starving.' Source: Chapter 12.

content.name: thirsty
content.side_e: Pá shān pá de hěn kě, mǎi píng shuǐ ba.
content.side_f: Got really thirsty hiking, let's buy a bottle of water.
content.side_g: 渴了 (kě le) means 'got thirsty.' Essential when hiking — you'll want to buy 水 (shuǐ, 'water') or 矿泉水 (kuàngquánshuǐ, 'mineral water'). Source: Chapter 12.

content.name: hot_weather
content.side_e: Jīntiān tài rè le! Hē diǎnr shuǐ ba.
content.side_f: It's too hot today! Drink some water.
content.side_g: Chongqing is called one of China's '四大火炉' (four great furnaces) — it gets extremely hot. 好热 (hǎo rè) means 'so hot.' 不热 (bú rè) means 'not hot.' Opposite: 冷 (lěng, 'cold'). Source: Chapter 11.

content.name: place
content.side_e: Zhè ge dìfang zhēn piàoliang!
content.side_f: This place is really beautiful!
content.side_g: Very useful for describing locations. 什么地方 (shénme dìfang) means 'what place/where.' 好地方 (hǎo dìfang) means 'great place.' Source: Chapter 13.

content.name: hike_climb_mountain
content.side_a: To hike; to climb a mountain
content.side_e: Wǒmen míngtiān qù pá shān ba!
content.side_f: Let's go hiking tomorrow!
content.side_g: 爬 means 'to climb/crawl' and 山 means 'mountain.' The standard expression for hiking or mountain climbing. In Zhangjiajie you'll hear this constantly. Use 去爬山 to say 'go hiking.' NEW travel word.

content.name: hot_pot
content.side_e: Chóngqìng de huǒguō fēicháng yǒumíng!
content.side_f: Chongqing's hot pot is extremely famous!
content.side_g: 火 means 'fire' and 锅 means 'pot.' Ask for 鸳鸯锅 (yuānyāng guō) for a split pot — half spicy, half mild. Chongqing's signature dish. NEW travel word.

content.name: spicy
content.side_e: Bú yào tài là de, wēi là jiù hǎo.
content.side_f: Don't make it too spicy, mildly spicy is fine.
content.side_g: 不辣 (bú là) means 'not spicy.' 微辣 'mildly spicy,' 中辣 'medium,' 特辣 'extra spicy.' In Chongqing, 麻辣 (málà) is the signature numbing-spicy flavor. NEW travel word.

content.name: delicious
content.side_e: Zhège huǒguō zhēn hǎochī!
content.side_f: This hot pot is really delicious!
content.side_g: 好 + 吃 = 'good to eat.' The best food compliment. 很好吃 and 真好吃 are intensified forms. For drinks: 好喝 (hǎohē). NEW travel word.

content.name: menu
content.side_e: Qǐng gěi wǒ càidān, xièxie.
content.side_f: Please give me the menu, thank you.
content.side_g: 请给我菜单 is the key phrase. Many restaurants now use QR codes — 扫码点餐 (sǎo mǎ diǎn cān, 'scan code to order'). NEW travel word.

content.name: restroom
content.side_e: Qǐngwèn, cèsuǒ zài nǎr?
content.side_f: Excuse me, where is the restroom?
content.side_g: 厕所在哪儿？is the essential phrase. Signs may say 卫生间 or 洗手间. On trails: 公共厕所 (public restroom). NEW travel word.

content.name: scenery
content.side_e: Zhāngjiājiè de fēngjǐng tài měi le!
content.side_f: The scenery in Zhangjiajie is so beautiful!
content.side_g: 风景区 (fēngjǐng qū) means 'scenic area' — you'll see this on signs everywhere. 风景很好 means 'the scenery is great.' NEW travel word.

content.name: travel_tourism
content.side_e: Wǒ hé wǒ tàitai lái Zhōngguó lǚyóu.
content.side_f: My wife and I came to China to travel.
content.side_g: Works as both verb and noun. 我来旅游的 (wǒ lái lǚyóu de) tells locals why you're visiting. 旅游景点 means 'tourist attraction.' NEW travel word.

content.name: take_photo
content.side_e: Kěyǐ bāng wǒmen zhào xiàng ma?
content.side_f: Can you help us take a photo?
content.side_g: 可以帮我们照相吗？is the key phrase for asking strangers to photograph you. Also: 拍照 (pāi zhào) is more colloquial. NEW travel word.

content.name: be_careful
content.side_e: Xiǎoxīn! Lù hěn huá!
content.side_f: Careful! The path is slippery!
content.side_g: Essential for hiking trails. 小心滑 means 'caution: slippery,' 小心台阶 means 'watch your step.' Literal meaning: 'small heart' = cautious. NEW travel word.
